topic: Zelenskyy Fires Ukraines Chief Protests Army

left.title: Zelenskyy fires Ukraine’s army chief and offers Fedorov ‘prominent position’

left-center.title: Ukraine’s Zelenskyy fires his commander in chief after protests and major rift

center.title: Ukraine's Zelenskyy fires his military chief after protests and names a new one

farLeftText: Volodymyr Zelenskyy has fired Ukraine’s commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi and offered a “prominent position” to the defence minister he sacked last week in a move that sparked nationwide protests. In a late-night TV address, Zelenskyy said he was appointing Mykhailo Drapatyi, a popular and innovative general, as the new head of Ukraine’s armed forces. Drapatyi would oversee a reorganisation of the army’s command structure, Ukraine’s president said. Zelenskyy said he was bringing Mykhailo Fedorov back into government after his removal as defence minister prompted thousands of people to demonstrate in Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities, in one of the biggest political crises since Russia’s 2022 invasion. View image in fullscreen Volodymyr Zelenskyy with Oleksandr Syrskyi at the handover of drone missiles to the Ukrainian army in Kyiv in December 2024. Photograph: Sergey Dolzhenko/EPA Ukraine’s president paid tribute to Syrskyi and credited him with leading the defence of Kyiv and launching a counter-invasion of Russia’s Kursk province in summer 2024, in which foreign tanks rolled on to Russian territory for the first time since the second world war. “We have come a long way. The defence of Ukraine continues. And every soldier deserves to be treated with respect,” Zelenskyy said, adding that he was grateful to Syrskyi and to “every one of our soldiers” fighting on the frontline. Zelenskyy will be hoping that his radical reshuffle of the army’s leadership will assuage protesters who have taken to the streets demanding Syrskyi’s removal. They had given a deadline of Friday for Fedorov to be reinstated and promised “indefinite protests” if their request was ignored. 1:32 Why Ukrainians are protesting against Zelenskyy’s dismissal of defence minister – video Fedorov welcomed Drapatyi’s appointment. “This brings a breath of fresh air and new hope to the struggle of free people for freedom and justice. It is a voice for change that could not be ignored,” he said. He added: “The high expectations of the Ukrainian people must be met with a clear vision for ending the war for independence, a strong team and decisive actions aimed at saving the lives of our soldiers, automating the frontline, delivering asymmetric strikes and crippling the Russian economy.” Drapatyi, the new commander-in-chief, is well-regarded within Ukraine’s army and is liked by the public. He is viewed as an ally of Fedorov, whose attempts to reform the military and force it to adopt modern technology and data led to a bitter clash with the old-school Syrskyi. A major general, Drapatyi is commander of Ukraine’s joint forces group. He has been fighting Russia since its covert attempt in 2014 to take over the eastern Donbas region. He became famous nationally when he drove an infantry fighting vehicle over a barricade set up by pro-Russian militants in the city of Mariupol. The Ukrainian media outlet Babel described Drapatyi as one of Ukraine’s most capable battlefield commanders. It said his new job would be fundamentally about reforming a large institution rather than commanding troops in combat. Zelenskyy said he and Drapatyi agreed that Ukraine’s long-range drone attacks on targets in Russia such as oil refineries would continue. “Our programme of precision strikes will be implemented with absolute precision,” he said, promising a smooth and coordinated handover following Syrskyi’s departure. He also said the new commander-in-chief would be tasked with updating the country’s defence strategy and reforming its structure. Another pressing issue is mobilisation, at a time when after nearly five years of full-scale war few Ukrainians are ready to volunteer. It is unclear what Fedorov’s new role will be, amid reports that he was insisting on being given his old job back as defence minister. This has now been filled by Yevhen Khmara, a former special operations commander. View image in fullscreen Yevhen Khmara replaced Mykhailo Fedeorov as Ukraine’s defence minister. Photograph: Ukrainian presidential press service/AFP/Getty Images Zelenskyy said he met Fedorov on Tuesday. “I offered Mykhailo a prominent position within the government that would enable him to unify the technological sector of our state and ensure its development,” he said, without giving further details. Zelenskyy added: “Our shared wish is one – victory over the enemy and conditions at the front and in pressure on Russia that would force Russia toward peace.” He said “a significant path has been travelled, the defence of Ukraine continues, and every warrior deserves dignified treatment”.
centerLeftText: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy fired his commander in chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi, on Tuesday, bowing to pressure after the removal of popular Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov sparked the fiercest leadership crisis of his presidency. Subscribe to read this story ad-free Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content. Zelenskyy announced on Telegram that Mykhailo Drapatyi will replace Syrskyi. Zelenskyy’s decision to fire Fedorov, one of his closest allies, led to rare wartime protests on the streets of Kyiv. Fedorov publicly aired his disagreements with Syrskyi, known as “The Butcher,” over military strategy. Syrskyi, 60, is a more traditional military commander focused on infantry and artillery, while Fedorov, 35, is seen as a modernizer, whose focus on technological advancements in drone warfare has led to Ukraine’s recent successes in hitting Russia. Despite the recent shake-up, Zelenskyy tried to present a unified front in his Telegram message Tuesday announcing the dismissal of Syrskyi. “The desire in all of us is one — victory over the enemy and achieving such conditions on the front and in pressure on Russia, which would allow forcing Russia to peace,” he wrote. Worried Ukrainians, fearing that Fedorov’s removal could wipe out those gains, took to the streets calling for him to be reinstated and Syrskyi to be forced out, instead. A senior air force commander also quit in protest. For those in the streets, Syrskyi had become a symbol of the Soviet way of thinking rejected by Ukraine’s younger generations. Fedorov, meanwhile, represents technological innovation and a determination to outsmart the country’s more powerful invader. Syrskyi has been nicknamed “The Butcher” for his unsparing reputation, with critics pointing to lives lost in a late withdrawal from the fortress city of Bakhmut and the disastrous retreat from Russia’s Kursk region last year that left soldiers stranded and forced to walk back for days under enemy fire. Zelenskyy spent the weekend speaking to other top military commanders in an apparent interview process to potentially replace Syrskyi. It is unclear whether Fedorov will remain in the government and, if so, in what capacity. “Change is inevitable,” Fedorov said Saturday on social media. “The dialogue is happening. I believe we will succeed.” Former Ukrainian Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov holds a briefing in Kyiv on Thursday. Danylo Antoniuk / Future Publishing via Getty Images Zelenskyy did not give a reason for firing Fedorov last week. He said he was effectively left with a choice between the defense minister and Syrskyi, who was heading the armed forces. The two were no longer communicating with each other, he said. Fedorov confirmed his spat with Syrskyi, saying Syrskyi was not willing to discuss “problems” directly and resisted reform of Ukraine’s defense apparatus. 00:22 Protests erupt in Ukraine over defense minister's ouster 00:00 00:00 While he credited Syrskyi for Ukraine’s wins in the early days of the war, “the war has changed” because of drone advancements, Fedorov argued. “Why is this trajectory breaking now, and why are we veering off course? There is such a risk. And the Ukrainian people have taken to the streets for that,” he said at a news conference. Ukraine’s deployment of low-cost drones has upended the battlefield and the traditional model of warfare, with Kyiv demonstrating a growing ability to hit strategic sites deep inside Russia, in addition to critical Russian-held targets within Ukraine’s territory. At the same time, Russian troops continue to slowly advance in eastern Ukraine, while a dwindling supply of missile interceptors has left Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities vulnerable to intense bombardment. On Tuesday, Zelenskyy separately met with Fedorov and offered him what he called a respected position in government that would unify the country’s technology sector. But it was not clear whether Fedorov accepted the offer and whether the latest moves would satisfy protesters and bring peace to the streets.
centerText:
Ukraine's Zelenskyy fires his military chief after protests and names a new one

toggle caption Efrem Lukatsky/AP

KYIV, Ukraine (AP) — President Volodymyr Zelenskyy on Tuesday fired Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi as commander-in-chief of Ukraine's armed forces following days of mass protests in Kyiv and other cities nationwide demanding his removal.

Zelenskyy appointed Mykhailo Drapatyi as the new army chief, according to a statement on social media. "Our shared wish is one — victory over the enemy and conditions at the front and in pressure on Russia that would force Russia toward peace," Zelenskyy said.

The Ukrainian leader said he made the decision after a series of meetings with military commanders. They began shortly after protests broke out demanding the reinstatement of former Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov, seen by demonstrators as a driver of military modernization, and the removal of Syrskyi, who they said embodied the army's old, Soviet-style command culture.

Sponsor Message

Zelenskyy thanked Syrskyi for his achievements in Ukraine's fight against Russia and praised him for the defense of Kyiv and for operations in Kharkiv and Kursk. "I am grateful to Oleksandr Syrskyi and to every one of our warriors for Ukraine's strong front-line positions," he said. "I am grateful to Mykhailo Drapatyi for holding that same view."

On Tuesday, Zelenskyy separately met with Fedorov and offered him what he called a respected position in government that would unify the country's technology sector. But it wasn't clear if Fedorov accepted the offer and whether the latest moves would satisfy protesters and bring peace to the streets.

topic: Appeals Court Rejects Ghostwriter Bidens Block

left-center.title: Appeals court rejects Biden’s bid to block release of recordings of ghostwriter interviews

right.title: Appeals Court Rejects Biden Effort to Shield Ghostwriter Interview Tapes

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText:
WASHINGTON — A federal appeals court has rejected a request by Joe Biden to block the release of audio recordings and transcripts of his interviews with a memoir ghostwriter before he was elected president.

Subscribe to read this story ad-free Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.

A divided three-judge panel from the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit suspended its decision until Aug. 3 to allow more time for Biden to consider another appeal.

The panel’s 2-1 ruling late Monday found there is a “substantial” public interest in disclosing the material that Biden wants to keep under wraps. Redactions to the recordings would help protect Biden’s privacy, the majority noted.

“We conclude that any remaining incursion on personal privacy from disclosure of the now-redacted materials likely does not outweigh the public interest in disclosure,” the court wrote.

Mark Zwonitzer, who worked with Biden on his two memoirs, interviewed the Democrat at his home in 2016 and 2017. Biden’s lawyers say the conversations were candid, personal and intended to remain private.

The recordings were obtained by special counsel Robert Hur, who investigated Biden’s handling of classified documents from his time as a senator from Delaware and as vice president. Republicans in Congress demanded the material after Hur declined to file charges against the then-president.

Biden sued and sought an injunction to prevent the Justice Department under President Donald Trump, a Republican, from releasing the recordings to Congress and the conservative Heritage Foundation. The department previously argued that the recordings were exempt from disclosure under public records law.

Biden appealed after U.S. District Judge Dabney Friedrich ruled in June that the public interest in the material outweighed whatever privacy rights Biden had. Friedrich was nominated by Trump.

The appeal was heard by D.C. Circuit Chief Judge Sri Srinivasan and Judges Gregory Katsas and Florence Pan.

Pan, who was nominated by Biden, dissented from the majority opinion.

Srinivasan was nominated by President Barack Obama, a Democrat who chose Biden as his vice president. Katsas was nominated by Trump.

Efforts by Joe Biden to block release of audio recordings and transcripts of interviews he gave to a memoir ghostwriter in 2017 ahead of his election to president were denied late Monday by a federal appeals court.

AP reports a divided three-judge panel from the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit suspended its decision until Aug. 3 to allow more time for Biden to consider another appeal.

The outlet notes the panel’s 2-1 ruling found there is a “substantial” public interest in disclosing the musings Biden wants to shield from view.

Redactions to the 70 hours of recordings would help protect Biden’s privacy, the majority noted.

“We conclude that any remaining incursion on personal privacy from disclosure of the now-redacted materials likely does not outweigh the public interest in disclosure,” the court wrote, per the AP.

Biden appealed after U.S. District Judge Dabney Friedrich ruled in June the public interest in the material outweighed whatever privacy rights Biden had.

Mark Zwonitzer, who worked with Biden on his two memoirs, interviewed the octogenarian Democrat at his home in 2016 and 2017. Biden’s lawyers maintain the conversations were candid, personal and intended to remain beyond public purview.

As Breitbart News reported, Biden will release his memoir this fall at a date set for two weeks after midterm elections in which Democrats seek to regain control of Congress.

The book’s publisher Little, Brown and Company told the Associated Press of the planned publication date for a tome the former president reportedly received a $10 million advance for.

It will be released on November 17.

More to come…

left.title: Hegseth tells Senate war on Iran has so far cost $37.5bn at hearing to request more funds

left-center.title: Hegseth tells senators Iran war has cost $37.5 billion so far

center.title: Iran war has cost US $37.5bn so far, Hegseth says

farLeftText: The US defense secretary, Pete Hegseth told the Senate on Tuesday that the US-Israel war on Iran had cost $37.5bn so far, as he faced lawmakers furious over the conflict’s soaring price tag and the deaths last week of three American service members. During an appearance before the Senate appropriations committee, Hegseth, whose opening remarks were repeatedly interrupted by protesters, provided an updated account of the war’s cost – up from previous estimates – and defended the Trump administration’s roughly $88bn supplemental funding request, calling it “urgent, necessary”. Alongside the chair of the joint chiefs, Gen Dan Caine, Hegseth argued that the Pentagon was running low on cash to keep the fight going. Of the total request, as much as $67.1bn is earmarked specifically to cover the costs of the Iran war effort. “Without these funds, we face critical shortfalls,” Hegseth told the Senate panel, warning that it would threaten the defense department’s ability to “pay our service members, rapidly replenish equipment and munitions, and sustain vital operations without disruption”. It was the defense secretary’s first sustained public questioning since a brief ceasefire collapsed and the war roared back to life. At least two US soldiers were killed in an Iranian attack on Friday, and another died in Iraq destroying an Iranian drone, pushing the American death toll since February to 17. Nearly 100 troops have been wounded in the conflict, officials confirm, though most injuries were minor. Patty Murray, the committee’s Democratic vice-chair, demanded Hegseth explain what the war had actually achieved, accusing the administration of shifting its rationale by the week and warning the conflict risked becoming “another forever war”. “We hear a different rationale for this war every few days,” Murray said in her opening remarks, noting the justification had moved from pre-empting threats that “did not exist” to regime change, then to dismantling Iran’s nuclear programme and navy, and now, she said, to simply reopening the strait of Hormuz, a waterway that was “fully open, toll-free, before this war began”. “Here we are, five months later”, she continued. “The strait is closed. The ceasefire hasn’t just disintegrated – this war is now spiraling out of control again.” Under questioning from lawmakers, Hegseth cast the funding request as the price of deterrence rather than admitting failure, and framed the Pentagon’s overhaul under the US president as a break from what he described as years of slow bureaucracy. “The best way to create peace is to prepare for war, to deter it,” Hegseth told the panel, arguing the department was “building a force so capable that it would be absurd for anyone to challenge it”. Hegseth said the $21bn readiness portion of the request would cover military pay, replace equipment used in recent operations and stabilize fuel shortfalls, while the $46bn capability portion would expand production of “solid rocket motors, Jdams [joint direct attack munitions], hypersonics and counter-drone capabilities”. Without the funds, he warned, the department faced “critical shortfalls” that threaten its ability to pay troops and replenish munitions, and he cautioned that failing to fund the Pentagon at $1.5tn was “the greatest threat that our nation faces”. Some Democrats, including Chris Coons of Delaware, plan to direct criticism at Russell Vought, director of office of management and budget, who is not testifying but who earlier told US Congress that the war’s running cost was about $30bn, a figure that is largely believed by experts to be a severe undercount. Hegseth and Caine faced potential threats from within their own party. Susan Collins of Maine, chair of the committee, and Alaska’s Lisa Murkowski, two prominent Republicans, have both crossed the aisle to back war-powers resolutions aimed at reining in the president’s authority over the conflict. Collins, who is facing a competitive re-election contest, has previously voiced frustration that the administration’s strategy for the war seems to shift almost daily. During the hearing, she asked Hegseth about the effect on military exercises if the supplemental funding is not provided. The defense secretary said that current and future training “would have to be curtailed” if the Pentagon does not get its budget needs met urgently. Senator John Kennedy, a Republican of Louisiana, pressed the panelists on the situation in the strait of Hormuz, asking if Iran will impose tolls on commercial vessels passing through the vital waterway which carried about a fifth of global oil and gas supplies before the war. “That’s a hypothetical question,” Caine replied, “and I don’t know the answer to that.” “Guys, we need straight answers,” Kennedy shot back. Both congressional chambers have passed a war-powers resolution urging the president to seek congressional authorization to continue military operations in Iran. Asked why the administration had not yet sought congressional approval for the war, Hegseth said it was the view of his department that “standalone Article II authorities exist for us to conduct this operation on behalf of the defense of the American people”. The defense secretary evaded the lawmaker’s question about whether he would use the approval of any supplemental funding as a “substitute for explicit congressional authorization”. House representatives have separately accused the Pentagon of stonewalling on basic figures: Betty McCollum, the top Democrat on the House defense appropriations panel, said the department has missed a deadline to detail war costs and has not disclosed how many precision munitions remain after the military burned through thousands of them in Iran.

centerText:
The war with Iran has cost the US some $37.5bn (£28bn) so far - an increase of nearly $8bn since the last estimate in May, Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth has said.

In his first appearance before the Senate Appropriations Committee since hostilities between the US and Iran restarted, Hegseth said that military training would have to be curtailed unless lawmakers approved an urgent funding boost.

He said he was seeking $87bn in congressional funding for his department, with $67bn of that destined for operations in the Middle East.

The hearing was interrupted several times by anti-war protesters, with some shouting "stop bombing our children in Iran and Palestine".

Hegseth's appearance before the committee came at a pivotal time in the war after three US soldiers were killed in the Middle East.

The White House asked Congress in April for $1.5tn for the Pentagon over the next fiscal year, which would raise American military spending to an all-time high in the modern era.

topic: Trump Direct Flights Lebanon Halt Allow

left.title: Trump to allow direct US flights to Lebanon after 41-year halt

center.title: Trump Authorizes Direct U.S. Flights to Lebanon: 4-Decade Halt Explained

right-center.title: Trump orders US airlines to resume direct flights to Lebanon after White House meeting with president

farLeftText: Donald Trump said on Tuesday he would allow American carriers to resume direct flights to Lebanon more than 40 years after the US suspended the route. Direct flights between the US and Lebanon were suspended in 1985 by Ronald Reagan’s administration following the hijacking of TWA flight 847. “I am hereby directing my Administration to allow all U.S. airline carriers to fly directly to Lebanon so that Americans can easily visit this beautiful land,” Trump said in a social media post after meeting with the Lebanese president, Joseph Aoun. During the meeting with Aoun, Trump pledged to help Lebanon “a lot”. Aoun came to Washington to push for long-term calm after months of war between Israel and the Iran-backed Hezbollah militant group. The US has pushed for peace in Lebanon but has been distracted by a new round of escalation in Iran, which has destabilized much of the Middle East. Trump recently said he had little interest in talks with Iran’s leader to end the war – at least for now. Lebanon and Israel have held rare direct talks mediated by Washington. Aoun’s White House visit was the first by a Lebanese president since 2009. “It’s been a very badly treated place and country and we’re going to have it properly treated and treated with the respect it deserves,” Trump told Aoun during the White House meeting, which wrapped up the Lebanese leader’s four-day visit to Washington. “We’re gonna help it a lot,” he said, without giving concrete details. Lebanon hopes the Washington talks result in Israeli troops withdrawing from large parts of southern Lebanon they currently occupy, and the Lebanese military receiving support to assert full control in areas where Hezbollah militants had held sway. Trump said of Israel’s army fully withdrawing from Lebanon: “They’re in the process of doing that. They’re in the process of redeploying.” He did not elaborate. Aoun told Trump: “Your vision is peace.” He called it Trump’s “legacy”.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText:
President Donald Trump announced Tuesday that he was calling for United States airlines to resume direct flights to Lebanon, after four decades without that option.

The president's announcement came after his meeting with Lebanese President Joseph Aoun, with the pair showing a mostly united front even as the U.S.'s key ally Israel continues its efforts to battle Houthis in the Middle Eastern country.

Trump said in a post on Truth Social that given Aoun's "remarkable job" in transforming Lebanon, "I am hereby directing my Administration to allow all U.S. airline carriers to fly directly to Lebanon so that Americans can easily visit this beautiful land. Hopefully, other Countries will do the same."

Read More on World

For Lebanon, restoring direct U.S. air links would carry symbolic weight beyond tourism revenue. It would represent one of the clearest signs in decades that Washington sees the country as stable enough to reconnect directly with the American aviation network, a relationship that was severed in the aftermath of a notorious 1980s airline hijacking.

...

Why Were Direct Flights Halted?

Direct commercial flights between the United States and Lebanon have effectively been suspended since 1985, when the Reagan administration moved to halt service following the hijacking of TWA Flight 847.

The Boeing 727 was seized after departing Athens and forced to Beirut, where passengers were held hostage for more than two weeks. One U.S. Navy diver, Robert Stethem, was killed during the ordeal. U.S. officials subsequently imposed restrictions that prevented direct air service between the two countries.

Can US Citizens Travel to Lebanon?

Even if the administration authorizes service, direct flights may not begin immediately.

Lebanon currently has a Level 4—Do Not Travel rating from the U.S. Department of State, meaning Americans are strongly advised not to head to the country.

Airlines would also need to determine whether sufficient passenger demand exists to support nonstop routes from cities such as New York, Detroit or Washington, D.C. Airlines would also need to navigate insurance, security, and operational considerations tied to the regional security situation.

According to aviation safety advisories, Israeli military operations and broader Middle East tensions continue to affect assessments of Lebanese airspace.

Reacting on X, international relations scholar Karim Emile Bitar called the announcement "welcome news" and "symbolically important," but said "caution is warranted." He wrote that previous efforts during the Bill Clinton and Barack Obama presidencies failed to produce direct flights to Beirut and pointed out that Lebanon remains under the State Department's highest travel warning. The ultimate decision, he said, lies with U.S. carriers, adding that "optics may be outrunning reality."

When Did Trump Host Lebanese President Joseph Aoun?

The announcement came during Aoun's visit to Washington on Tuesday and can be seen as part of a broader effort to strengthen ties between the two countries after years of instability in Lebanon.

Trump described Aoun as having done a "remarkable job" transforming the country, after the pair held a press briefing in the Oval Office, during which the U.S. president was asked about the ongoing conflict in Iran.

Trump appeared skeptical of further talks with Iran, telling reporters, "I will tell you, they want to desperately meet. And until they’re ready to meet in a meaningful way, we have no interest in meeting."

Iran and the U.S. escalated attacks in recent days, with their interim deal to end the war falling apart.

Aoun was expected to ask Trump to pressure Israel to implement a U.S.-brokered agreement in which Israel Defense Forces (IDF) troops would be drawn down from "pilot zones" ahead of a wider withdrawal.

Is There Actually Demand for US-Lebanon Flights?

While Lebanon is not a major destination for mainstream American tourism, there is a substantial Lebanese-American community that could provide a built-in customer base for nonstop flights.

The 2020 Census counted roughly 686,000 Americans of Lebanese ancestry, making Lebanese Americans the largest single Middle Eastern-origin group recorded by the Census. Other estimates place the figure at more than 1 million. Major Lebanese-American populations are concentrated in Michigan, New York, California, Florida, Texas, and New Jersey.

Travel industry observers have long viewed Lebanon as a strong "visiting friends and relatives" market. Much of the country's tourism sector relies on expatriates and dual nationals returning to see family, particularly during summer months. In 2022, Lebanese nationals and dual citizens accounted for roughly 63 percent of inbound visitors to Lebanon.
centerRightText:
NEW You can now listen to Fox News articles!

President Donald Trump on Tuesday announced he is directing his administration to allow all U.S. airlines to fly to Lebanon after a White House meeting with Lebanese President Joseph Aoun, saying the move will make it easier for Americans to visit the country.

In a Truth Social post after the meeting, Trump praised Aoun for "working to transform his Country" and said he was directing his administration to allow all U.S. airline carriers to "fly directly to Lebanon so that Americans can easily visit this beautiful land."

"After meeting with the President of Lebanon, Joseph Aoun, who has done a remarkable job working to transform his Country, I am hereby directing my Administration to allow all U.S. airline carriers to fly directly to Lebanon so that Americans can easily visit this beautiful land," Trump wrote. "Hopefully, other Countries will do the same. Enjoy!"

IRAN-BACKED PROXIES SABOTAGING LEBANON PEACE AS JD VANCE LEADS US DELEGATION TO SWITZERLAND, HERZOG SAYS

Speaking alongside Aoun in the Oval Office earlier Tuesday, Trump called Lebanon "a country of great intellect" and vowed the U.S. would "help it a lot," while hinting at additional moves beyond his announcement on direct flights.

"We've already solved some of them for Lebanon," Trump said. "As you know, there's a Hezbollah problem. But we've done some things that I think the world will take notice."

The announcement marks another step in the Trump administration's efforts to strengthen ties with Lebanon after months of U.S.-backed diplomacy aimed at reducing Hezbollah's influence and increasing regional stability.

Secretary of Transportation Sean Duffy took to social media after the president's announcement, writing on X, "You got it, Mr. President."

The announcement comes after months of U.S.-backed diplomacy between Lebanon and Israel as the Trump administration has sought to reduce Hezbollah's influence and bolster regional stability.

ISRAEL–HEZBOLLAH CEASEFIRE BECOMES FIRST TEST OF TRUMP IRAN FRAMEWORK AFTER TALKS DELAY

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu called the agreement "a severe blow to Iran," while U.S. Ambassador to Israel Mike Huckabee described it as a "historic opportunity," adding that disarming Hezbollah remained essential to any lasting peace.

Aoun thanked the Trump administration for helping facilitate negotiations and supporting Lebanon during the talks.

The White House did not immediately respond to Fox News Digital's request for additional comment, including on the timeline for implementing the policy.

topic: Trump Tariff Canadian Goods Impose Donald

left.title: Donald Trump to impose 50% tariff on most Canadian goods, White House says

left-center.title: Trump to Impose 50% Tariff on Many Canadian Goods

right.title: Trump Imposes 50 Percent Tariff on Variety of Canadian Goods Over 'Discrimination' of American Autos

farLeftText: Donald Trump is imposing 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods in response to the country retaliating against previous US tariffs, the White House announced on Monday, declaring Canada has unfairly discriminated against American cars, alcohol and dairy products. The tariffs will hit a wide range of products, the White House said, including wine, hockey sticks and cement. They also include goods previously protected from import taxes under the United States-Mexico-Canada (USMCA) agreement. The new tariffs will exclude energy products, fish, critical minerals and potash. They also exclude products already subject to tariffs aimed at protecting national security, such as steel and aluminum. The steep tariffs will probably unleash a new wave of economic chaos, with risks of higher inflation and further fraying of relations between two nations that had been closely woven together before Trump’s return to the White House. The Canadian prime minister, Mark Carney, said in a statement that his government had made comprehensive proposals ‌to resolve trade disputes with Washington, asserting that Trump’s past tariffs violated ‌a trade pact between the two countries. “This trade dispute has raised costs for families, particularly in the US,” he said. “Canada stands ready to engage intensively to address outstanding issues with the US to the mutual benefit of our citizens.” The White House said the tariffs will go into effect in 30 days, leaving time for possible negotiations between the two countries. Trump signed three proclamations to launch the tariffs under Section 338 of the 1930 Trade Act. Several Democratic lawmakers last year proposed repealing the section because they said Trump could use it to destabilize the economy. A White House fact sheet claims Trump is “taking action to hold Canada accountable for its continued discrimination against and unreasonable and unequal treatment of U.S. commerce that has burdened and disadvantaged hardworking Americans”. Trump claims in the proclamations that Canada discriminates against American automobiles, alcohol and cheese relative to other nations, but his argument rests in large part on retaliatory actions taken by Canada after the US president imposed tariffs on Canada under the pretext that it should do more to stop fentanyl smuggling into the US. Trump noted in his autos proclamation that Canada maintained, starting in April 2025, a 25% tariff on the imports of US motor vehicles that did not qualify for preferential treatment under the USMCA. The White House also cited all but two Canadian provinces and territories stopping the sale of US alcoholic beverages, while not imposing similar restrictions on other countries. Trump also objected to Canada’s treatment of US cheese, saying in his proclamation that Canada discriminates against the US compared with Europe on dairy products. Trump had threatened last week to impose tariffs due to smoke from devastating wildfires in Canada drifting across large portions of the US. “We are holding Canada responsible for the fact that they are not properly maintaining their Forests, and Brush therein, and the United States is being unnecessarily invaded by filthy, polluted, and unhealthy air, the quality of which is dangerous, and totally unacceptable!” he wrote on Truth Social. Trump watched Sunday’s World Cup final with Mark Carney, the Canadian prime minister who has previously pushed back on the US president and won the premiership last year on a platform of promising to stand up for Canada. After the match, Trump told reporters he had raised the issue of wildfire smoke with Carney. skip past newsletter promotion Free newsletter | Around 2-3 times a day Sign up to Breaking News US Get the most important news as it breaks Preview latest Enter your email Sign up after newsletter promotion “I told him, I mean, ‘You got to stop these fires from coming in and, you know, poisoning our air. Our air has been poisoned,’” Trump said of their conversation. A White House official who previewed the new tariffs to reporters said Trump had requested aides explore additional tariffs due to Canadian wildfires harming US air quality, the Associated Press reported. The Canadian federal government did not immediately comment on Trump’s action, but Doug Ford, the Ontario premier, said he would “never stop fighting to protect Ontario”. “If these tariffs proceed, Canada should respond tariff for tariff, dollar for dollar,” Ford posted on social media. Candace Laing, the CEO of the Canadian chamber of commerce, said the Trump administration’s moves were “regrettable” but the two countries needed to use the 30-day window before the tariffs start “to make meaningful progress in advancing formal talks”. The new taxes on Canadian imports could carry serious political and economic risks for Trump ahead of the November midterm elections for control of Congress. His so-called “liberation day” tariffs last year in April provoked a financial market meltdown over concerns about inflation and a recession, prompting him to walk back the rates for a period of negotiation. In a 6-3 decision in February, the supreme court ruled that Trump had illegally used emergency executive power to impose his global tariffs, causing the administration to find alternative ways to raise import taxes based on a series of legal authorities. The US has been forced to pay back $81bn in tariffs so far this fiscal year. The Associated Press contributed reporting

farRightText: President Donald Trump on Monday imposed a 50 percent duty on a variety of Canadian goods, including alcohol, dairy products, hockey sticks, and many others. The White House published the proclamation late in the afternoon, and the duties the United States is imposing are through Section 228 of the Tariff Act of 1930. A White House fact sheet lays out some of the Canadian products that are affected: Each Section 338 proclamation imposes a 50% tariff on a different set of Canadian imports, covering products ranging from wine to hockey sticks to cement. These Section 338 tariffs apply to all covered goods regardless of whether a good originates under the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA). These Section 338 tariffs will not apply to energy, potash, products subject to tariffs under Section 232, and certain other goods, such as fish or critical minerals. The tariffs will take effect 30 days after signing and are designed to offset the burden and disadvantage on U.S. commerce from Canada’s discrimination. ABC News provided further details on the products now facing a 50 percent duty: The list of goods covered by the new duties is very wide-ranging, from dairy products, alcohol and alcohol-related products to some food products. The tariffs also cover construction materials, clothing, furniture, technology and car parts. The proclamation emphasizes the tariffs are in response to “discrimination” via a Canadian “tariff scheme” against American U.S. motor vehicles and auto parts. “Specifically, Canada imposed a tariff system on only U.S. motor vehicles and treats the commerce of foreign countries more favorably than commerce of the United States with respect to motor vehicles, as defined in Canada’s United States Surtax Order…” the proclamation states. “I find that imposing additional ad valorem duties on certain products of Canada to address the burden or disadvantage from this discrimination or unequal and unreasonable imposition is in the public interest, will serve the public interest, and is consistent with the interests of the United States.” The move comes a day after Trump was with Prime Minister Mark Carney at the World Cup Final between Spain and Argentina at MetLife Stadium in Rutherford, New Jersey. Trump also noted last week he would be imposing tariffs on Canada to compensate for costs incurred from Canadian wildfire smoke that blanketed the United States.

Secretary of War Pete Hegseth told the Senate Appropriations Committee on Tuesday that the department’s new estimated cost for the Iran war is up to $37.5 billion.

That $37.5 billion estimate from Hegseth, which he said includes some additional military pay and operations and maintenance costs anticipated through the fiscal year, is up from the department’s last estimate of $29 billion in May.

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: Roughly 6,600 noncitizens were unwittingly registered to vote in New Jersey from 2023 to 2024, Gov. Mikie Sherrill said Tuesday. Subscribe to read this story ad-free Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content. Roughly 400 of them improperly cast votes, she said. “They were registered as Democrats, Republicans and unaffiliated voters and were scattered across the state,” said Sherrill, a Democrat. She added that it is unclear which elections they voted in and said there was “no evidence at this time that any elections were swayed” as a result. Sherrill said that the names are being removed from the voter rolls and that the noncitizens had been registered through no fault of their own because of “a serious software error in New Jersey’s Motor Vehicle System” from June 2023 to June 2024. “These individuals answered ‘no’ when asked on a keypad whether they were a U.S. citizen when applying for drivers’ licenses and identification cards, but through no fault of their own, the system registered them anyway,” Sherrill said. While the number of noncitizens who voted in New Jersey is a tiny percentage of the state’s overall electorate, the finding could add fuel to President Donald Trump’s claims that they are voting in large numbers in U.S. elections. He used a primetime speech from the White House last week to push Congress to pass the SAVE America Act, which would impose new photo ID and proof-of-citizenship requirements to vote. White House spokeswoman Abigail Jackson said in a statement that the New Jersey incident “underscores the absolute necessity of the SAVE America Act.” “Democrats and their media allies have repeatedly said it is impossible for noncitizens to register to vote, let alone cast a ballot. Time and again, they have been proven wrong,” Jackson said. Sherrill said in a statement that “Donald Trump has zero credibility on the issue of election integrity.” While his administration “tries to weaponize elections for political gain,” she said, “I am ensuring we protect our elections.” Later on Tuesday, Assistant Attorney General Harmeet Dhillon said in a letter posted to X that the Justice Department is investigating the incident. It’s asking for the full names, dates of birth, nationalities, addresses, and registration dates of all 6,600 noncitizens, and for the 400 people who Sherrill says voted in past elections, including when and where they voted. “Ensuring that U.S. citizens’ votes are not illegally diluted by noncitizens’ votes is of paramount importance,” Dhillon wrote in her letter to Sherrill. Experts say that this type of error does occur rarely, affecting thousands in a country with nearly 300 million registrants. Still, the scale of the mistake in New Jersey goes beyond recent examples in other states. In 2024, Oregon officials announced they had uncovered an administrative error that put more than 1,200 noncitizens on the state’s voter rolls. And in 2018, California officials said they may have registered 1,500 noncitizens accidentally. The number of mistaken registrants who cast ballots is typically quite small: In Oregon, officials said, nine people who had been mistakenly registered appeared to have cast a ballot. Sherrill, who took office in January, said the mistake in New Jersey happened during former Democratic Gov. Phil Murphy’s tenure. She said it was “unacceptable that no one in the prior administration brought this to light, demanded accountability or took action when it happened years ago.” Murphy did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Sherrill said she found out about the issue Wednesday — two days before Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin alleged in a letter that 35,000 noncitizens were registered to vote in the state. The Department of Homeland Security gave the state two weeks to respond and confirm its “intentions to collaborate with DHS in order to ensure free, fair, and honest elections.” Sherrill said the state has asked for evidence of Mullin’s claim and “heard nothing back.” She added she does not intend to turn personally identifying information over to the federal government because the Trump administration “continues to weaponize it against people.” Mullin responded to a social media post in which Sherrill divulged the error, saying, “Appreciate you responding back to our letter.” “Looking forward to working with you to secure your elections in the future,” he said on X. Sherrill indicated in her statement that her office would deal with the issue itself. “When we find a problem, we don’t hide it, deny it, or invent conspiracies. We investigate it, we fix it, and we tell the public. That’s what accountability and good governance look like, and that’s exactly what my administration is doing,” she said. She said her office has launched its own investigation and “will be holding people accountable.” They do not include those who were registered mistakenly. Sherrill said that she has concerns that their immigration status could be affected as a result of the mistake and that it would be “the utmost in hypocrisy for the state to charge them when it’s our fault.” She also said officials are replacing the vendor responsible for the error, Idemia, “to make sure nothing like this happens again.” The company did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
centerText: The Democratic governor, who took office this year, blamed a Motor Vehicle Commission system software error but said a preliminary analysis showed that fewer than 400 of the people registered that way went on to cast ballots. About 6,600 people who indicated they were noncitizens while obtaining New Jersey driver's licenses and other state IDs were improperly registered to vote in 2023 and 2024, Gov. Mikie Sherrill said on Tuesday. The revelation comes as President Donald Trump and other Republicans continue to assert that voting by noncitizens is rampant in U.S. elections, even though it's rare and, when caught, can be punished as a felony that can lead to deportation. WATCH: How the Trump administration is trying to reshape how elections are run Sherrill said she has ordered that erroneously added voter registrations from that 2023-24 period be removed and is replacing the vendor who ran the system. She also is launching an investigation into how the error happened. "I am appalled by the reckless failures that allowed this to happen and the lack of transparency shown by those in charge at the time," she said in a statement. Phil Murphy, also a Democrat, was governor then. The Associated Press was making efforts to contact him or his representatives. New Jersey is among several states that allow noncitizens to hold driver's licenses and other state-issued ID cards.

farLeftText: From 39m ago 19.14 EDT Trump announces still more tariffs, this time on generic drugs Donald Trump announced a new package of tariffs on Tuesday, this time on generic drugs made overseas, to begin in 2028. The announcement, in a social media post, came just three months after the president issued a proclamation saying that generic pharmaceuticals “are not subject to tariffs at this time” but that decision “will be reassessed in one year.” Trump’s latest head-spinning reversal on tariff policy means, he said, that generic drugs will continue to be imported tariff-free until 1 August 2028, when a 100% tariff would be imposed “for a one year period of time, and 200% thereafter.” The goal of the policy, Trump said, was to coerce drug makers into building plants in the United States. Trump did not state what legal authority he was claiming to impose future tariffs on generic drugs, particularly the projected 200% tariff which would come into effect in August 2029, six months after the end of his final term. Share 8m ago 19.44 EDT Democrats say they will repeal Trump's tariffs As Donald Trump announces new tariffs daily, leading one sober trade expert to observe, “Tariff Crazytown is officially BACK”, Democratic lawmakers are lining up to promise to repeal the Trump tariffs as soon as they regain control of Congress. “Democrats will flip the House. And on Day 1 of next term, Democrats will introduce legislation to repeal trump’s disastrous tariffs. And we will pass it,” congressman Ted Lieu, a California Democrat, wrote after Trump imposed 50% tariffs on a range of goods from Canada on Monday, citing Section 338 of the disastrous Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act of 1930 as his legal authority. “Donald Trump has abused every trade authority at his disposal, and he is now excavating a law from the Great Depression to once again impose vast, unilateral tariffs on products from one of our closest allies and trading partners,” senator Ron Wyden, an Oregon Democrat, said in a statement. “This is yet another shakedown that will raise the cost-of-living for Americans, their families, and small businesses across the country. Trump cannot be trusted with tariff authority, and I will be introducing a bill very soon to rein him in and put Congress back in the driver’s seat.” In a Substack column republished by the Toronto Star on Tuesday, the Nobel-winning economist Paul Krugman wrote: double quotation mark The Trade Act of 1930, generally known as the Smoot-Hawley tariff, lost its standing as the worst trade policy action in US history when Donald Trump imposed his “Liberation Day” tariffs in April 2025. But Smoot-Hawley was widely recognized as a terrible mistake soon after its passage, as it provoked widespread retaliation and contributed to the downward spiral of world trade that accompanied and reinforced the Great Depression. … Yesterday the Trump administration invoked an obscure and never-before-used provision of Smoot-Hawley — Section 338 — to impose 50 percent tariffs on a wide range of Canadian goods. … Whatever the real motivation for these new tariffs, they are almost surely illegal. They are definitely a violation of the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade agreement, a revision of NAFTA negotiated and signed by Trump himself during his first term … So these latest actions confirm what most people around the world, from the European Commission to Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard, have already figured out: a deal with Donald Trump lasts only until he feels like breaking it. Share 1h ago 18.30 EDT Iran war has cost $37.5bn so far, Hegseth tells Senate The US-Israeli war on Iran has cost the Pentagon $37.5bn so far, defense secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate Appropriations Committee on Tuesday. At the Senate hearing, which has now concluded, senator Dick Durbin, an Illinois Democrat, asked Hegseth to explain why the Pentagon was asking Congress for an additional $67bn to pay for the war Trump started on 28 February when “the Department of Defense still has $75bn in unobligated funding from last year’s reconciliation package”. US defense secretary Pete Hegseth said that the US war on Iran had cost the Pentagon $37.5 “That 75,” Hegseth replied, “there’s plans and designs on that part of the rebuild of our military which had to be undertaken.” Hegseth then launched into a deeply partisan attack on his predecessor, Lloyd Austin, the retired army general, for supposedly pursuing “woke priorities” instead of military readiness. “I don’t know what Lloyd Austin did for four years, I really don’t,” he went on. “The amount of neglect, the amount of stale thinking and bureaucracy proceed staggering when we took over.” “Let me interrupt your speech for a minute, Mr Secretary,” Durbin cut in. “You estimated at one point that the cost of the war in Iran was $29bn, but then added a caveat that there was a replacement of munitions, for example. Do you have a new estimate about what the war has cost us so far in this the fifth month of the first year of this war in Iran?” the senator asked. “Senator, the estimate we have, as of today, is $37.5bn,” Hegseth answered. Pressed further by Durbin, Hegseth said that was the estimated cost of the war on Iran through the end of the US government’s financial year on 30 September. Share Updated at 18.35 EDT 2h ago 17.59 EDT Voting rights expert calls New Jersey voter registration glitch 'miniscule' The White House called on Tuesday for the House and Senate to pass the Republican SAVE America Act, which would require proof of citizenship to register to vote, and photo ID to cast a ballot, after New Jersey’s governor, Mikie Sherrill, revealed that a computer software error in the state’s automatic voter registration system for licensed drivers mistakenly registered about 6,600 people who identified themselves as non-US citizens in 2023 and 2024. According to Sherrill, who was not in office at the time, “fewer than 400 individuals who were newly registered for the first time because of the error voted. They were registered as Democrats, Republicans and Unaffiliated voters and were scattered across the state.” David Becker, a voting rights expert who leads the nonprofit Center for Election Innovation & Research, put the size of the glitch into context in a statement sent to reporters. “While administrative errors such as New Jersey’s do infrequently occur, they are very rare and affect a very small fraction of voters. The states entrusted to run our elections are best positioned to detect and correct these errors, as they have access to substantially better data than the federal government,” Becker said. “As CEIR’s own research affirms, noncitizen registration remains extremely rare, and noncitizen voting is infinitesimally rare. In this case, the number of wrongful registrations represents a miniscule fraction, roughly 0.1%, of New Jersey’s total active voter registration records in 2024,” he continued. “The number of potential ballots cast from those incorrect registrations would represent roughly 0.009% of all ballots cast in New Jersey’s 2024 general election, when Kamala Harris defeated President Trump by more than 250,000 votes,” Becker added. “Joe Biden actually defeated Trump by a margin three times as large before this error took place.” Share Updated at 18.23 EDT 3h ago 17.09 EDT Hegseth refuses to say that US military has ability to destroy Iranian facility buried beneath Pickaxe mountain At the ongoing Senate Appropriations Committee hearing, senator John Kennedy, a Louisiana Republican, pressed US defense secretary Pete Hegseth to say whether the US military could bomb a fortified underground site linked to Iran’s nuclear program known as Pickaxe mountain, which the US has not yet targeted. “We need straight answers,” Kennedy said with some exasperation. “Do we have the ability to destroy whatever is underneath Pickaxe mountain?” “Senator, a lot of our capabilities are classified, but if anybody in the world can reach anything on God’s given, God’s green Earth, it’s the United States military,” Hegseth replied. Kennedy’s question was perhaps triggered by reports that experts suggest the site is beyond ​the reach of the most powerful bunker ⁠buster bombs in ​the US arsenal. Donald Trump was asked by a reporter earlier on Tuesday if he believes an Israeli intelligence assessment that Iran has moved nuclear centrifuges into tunnels deep inside the mountain, called Kolang Gaz La in Persian, which is about a mile from Iran’s badly damaged ⁠Natanz uranium enrichment ​facility. “I think that they may have; we don’t have it on record,” Trump replied, before veering into a digression to cast the speculation about the mountain as possibly flawed reporting from news outlets. “All we do is read the fake news, you know, we see… the news is so wrong so many times, so you know, maybe this one they have right.” “It doesn’t mean anything unless they have the material. You’re talking about the centrifuges,” Trump added. “We follow the material, that’s where the action is, and we’ll be hitting that area very, probably pretty soon and very heavily.” According to the nonpartisan Institute for Science and International Security think tank in Washington, this site was “not visibly attacked” by US bombers in 2025, when US bombers dropped two two GBU-57 bunker buster bombs on the nearby nuclear facility at Natanz. “Iran announced in 2020 that the purpose of the Pickaxe tunnel complex is to hold an advanced centrifuge assembly facility that can produce several thousand centrifuges a year,” the institute reported last year. “In addition, based on the estimated size of the underground halls, the Institute several years ago assessed that site could also hold a centrifuge enrichment facility.” Share 3h ago 16.57 EDT Chris Stein The Senate intelligence committee on Tuesday advanced the nomination of Jay Clayton to serve as the US intelligence director, despite criticism from Democrats. Donald Trump had nominated Clayton, now the top federal prosecutor in the southern district of New York, for the role of the director of national intelligence (DNI) last month after the departure of Tulsi Gabbard. Intelligence committee members questioned Clayton at a hearing last week, and its Republican chair, Tom Cotton, announced that it had reported his nomination favorably by a 9-8 vote, teeing him up for a future vote on his confirmation by the full Senate. “Jay Clayton is a highly qualified nominee with a deep experience combatting a wide range of national security threats,” Cotton said in a statement. “I’m pleased that the Senate Intelligence Committee voted to advance his nomination to be Director of National Intelligence,” he added. Cotton did not provide a breakdown of how lawmakers voted. The committee is composed of nine Republicans and eight Democrats, and a spokesperson for the Democratic vice-chair, Mark Warner, said he had opposed the nomination. At his hearing last week, members of the Democratic minority criticized Clayton, a former Wall Street attorney who served as chair of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), for refusing to say that Joe Biden won the 2020 election. He also faced questions about previous statements he made echoing Trump’s claim that US elections have been marred by fraud, and concerns about politicization at the office of the DNI, which serves to coordinate the nation’s intelligence agencies. Senate committee advances nomination of Trump intelligence pick Jay Clayton Read more Share 4h ago 16.20 EDT 'Air power's got limits' top US military commander says when pressed on whether strikes on Iran can end war At the Senate Appropriations Committee hearing, Dan Caine, the chairman of the joint chiefs of staff, was asked by senator Jack Reed, a Rhode Island Democrat and veteran who previously taught economics and international relations at West Point, whether Iran can be deterred and defeated by “air strikes alone”. “I think, academically, as you look across the totality of history, air power’s got limits, and we always have to be mindful of those,” Caine responded. But now he is asking for 70 billion dollars more, and for us to just trust him.” Share 5h ago 14.47 EDT The hearing where defense secretary Pete Hegseth and chairman of the joint chiefs of staff Dan Caine will testify before Senate appropriations lawmakers, and explain their request for $80bn in supplemental funding for the Iran, has started. The committee chair – Republican senator Susan Collins – kicked off by noting that “irrespective of our differing views about the military operations”, there are “must-pay bills and urgent requirements facing the department that affect the ability of our troops to be well trained”. Collins – a noted moderate who is facing a reelection battle in her state of Maine – also pointed to “deeply troubling” reports that dozens of senior officers removed from promotion lists, many of whom appear to women and people of color. “This is likely to discourage well-qualified women and individuals of color from joining our armed forces, thus depriving us of their talent, dedication, and patriotism,” Collins said in her opening remarks. “The United States military is one of the greatest meritocracies in the free world. We need to make sure that it stays that way.” Share Updated at 16.08 EDT

topic: Senate Committee Advances Nomination Clayton Trump

left.title: Senate committee advances nomination of Trump intelligence pick Jay Clayton

right.title: Senate Committee Advances Clayton DNI Nomination

farLeftText: The Senate intelligence committee on Tuesday advanced the nomination of Jay Clayton to serve as the US intelligence director, despite criticism from Democrats. Donald Trump had nominated Clayton, now the top federal prosecutor in the southern district of New York, for the role of the director of national intelligence (DNI) last month after the departure of Tulsi Gabbard. Intelligence committee members questioned Clayton at a hearing last week, and its Republican chair, Tom Cotton, announced that it had reported his nomination favorably by a 9-8 vote, teeing him up for a future vote on his confirmation by the full Senate. “Jay Clayton is a highly qualified nominee with a deep experience combatting a wide range of national security threats,” Cotton said in a statement. “I’m pleased that the Senate Intelligence Committee voted to advance his nomination to be Director of National Intelligence,” he added. Cotton did not provide a breakdown of how lawmakers voted. The committee is composed of nine Republicans and eight Democrats, and Mark Warner, a spokesperson for the Democratic vice-chair, said he had opposed the nomination. Democratic senator Ron Wyden released a statement announcing his no vote, accusing Clayton of “spreading conspiracies” about mail-in voting and denying Joe Biden’s victory in the 2020 election. “From start to finish, Mr Clayton has proven himself incapable of telling the truth or demonstrating an iota of independence from Donald Trump,” Wyden said. At his hearing last week, members of the Democratic minority criticized Clayton, a former Wall Street attorney who served as the chair of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), for previous statements he made echoing Trump’s claim that US elections have been marred by fraud. They also aired concerns about politicization at the office of the DNI, which serves to coordinate the nation’s intelligence agencies. It is unclear when his nomination will be voted on by the Senate. If confirmed, he would take over from Bill Pulte, a federal housing official who Trump appointed as acting DNI following Gabbard’s departure. Democrats strenuously objected to Pulte’s installation in that office, decrying him as a loyalist to the president who may try to use its powers to retaliate against his enemies. As director of the Federal Housing Finance Agency director, Pulte had last year referred several Democratic officeholders as well as the Federal Reserve governor Lisa Cook for mortgage fraud. His predecessor, Gabbard, had raised similar concerns when she appeared alongside FBI agents who raided an election office in Georgia earlier this year. Administration officials later said she was running her own investigation of the 2020 election. Clayton’s confirmation may nonetheless clear a path for Congress to renew the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (Fisa), a key spy tool that had its legal authorization expire last month in part due to a blockade by Democrats incensed by Trump’s appointment of Pulte. Since taking over as acting DNI, Pulte has reportedly fired dozens of staffers from the office. In his statement, Cotton signaled support for those purges, saying he looked forward to working with Clayton to “continue reforming the Office of Director of National Intelligence”.
